text: The railway clerk explains that ledgers are exacting, but he needs a ticket, date, destination, or passenger name before he can help.
The analyst can identify a substance only if you bring a sample, stain, vessel, or exact preparation.
The insurance clerk cannot search the books without a policy number, insured name, or particular claim.
The dock clerk handles hundreds of crates a day. He needs a ship, warehouse, cargo mark, policy number, or consignee.
The registry can trace a cab by number, driver, or unusually precise journey, but not by rumour alone.
The pawnbroker keeps his books, but he cannot identify a pledge without a receipt, object, initials, or date.
The publican has plenty of ordinary gossip, but nothing that appears to touch the present matter.
The reading room can provide background knowledge, but this case calls for more specific evidence.
You make enquiries, but nothing here appears to advance the present case.
